title: Mitochondria
definition: Double-membrane-bound organelles responsible for generating most of the cell's supply of adenosine triphosphate (ATP).
mechanism: Utilizes the Electron Transport Chain (ETC) to create a proton gradient that drives ATP synthesis.
biological_function: Produces the metabolic heat and infrared energy felt as 'warmth' during focused intent.

title: Sodium-Potassium Pump (Na+/K+-ATPase)
definition: An active transport protein that moves 3 sodium ions out of the cell and 2 potassium ions in against their concentration gradients.
mechanism: Consumes ATP to flip its internal structure, physically pushing ions across the membrane to reset the electrical potential.
biological_function: Maintains the -70mV resting potential, keeping the neuron 'loaded' and ready to fire.

title: Potassium Leak Channels
definition: Passive trans-membrane proteins that allow potassium ions to slowly exit the cell along their chemical gradient.
mechanism: They remain open at rest, allowing K+ to 'leak' out, which prevents weak electrical signals from reaching the firing threshold.
biological_function: Acts as the brain's noise-cancellation system, silencing background neural static.

title: SNARE Proteins
definition: A large family of proteins that mediate the docking and fusion of synaptic vesicles with the target membrane.
mechanism: Complementary proteins on the vesicle and membrane entwine like a zipper, pulling the membranes together to form a fusion pore.
biological_function: Enables the near-instantaneous release of neurotransmitters in response to Calcium influx.

title: Calcium-Calmodulin Complex
definition: The structural union formed when Calcium ions bind to the protein Calmodulin, triggering a shape-change.
mechanism: Once bound, the complex physically wraps around other proteins (like eNOS) to activate or inhibit them.
biological_function: The bridge between the electrical signal (Calcium) and the physical result (Vasodilation).

title: NO/cGMP Signaling Pathway
definition: A major signaling cascade where Nitric Oxide triggers the production of cyclic Guanosine Monophosphate.
mechanism: NO gas diffuses into smooth muscle cells, activating guanylyl cyclase, which produces cGMP to tell muscle fibers to 'let go'.
biological_function: Triggers the widening of blood vessels (vasodilation) to flood tissues with oxygen and fuel.

title: Electron Transport Chain (ETC)
definition: A series of protein complexes in the mitochondrial membrane that transfer electrons via redox reactions.
mechanism: Electrons are passed through four complexes, pumping protons across the membrane to create a high-pressure energy gradient.
biological_function: The core site of ion oscillation and electromagnetic field generation in the body.

title: Polarization & Voltage
definition: The difference in electrical charge between the inside and outside of a cell membrane.
mechanism: Uneven ion distribution creates an electrical gradient (usually -70mV at rest) similar to a charged capacitor.
biological_function: Provides the 'voltage' necessary for rapid signal transmission through the nervous system.

title: Ampère–Maxwell Law
definition: An electromagnetic principle stating that a changing electric field or a moving electric current induces a magnetic field.
mechanism: The rhythmic flipping of trillions of ions (current) generates an oscillating electromagnetic field surrounding the tissue.
biological_function: Explains how the biological process of ion oscillation manifests as a measurable Bio-field or Aura.

title: Cellular Polarization
definition: The process of maintaining a distinct charge separation across a biological membrane.
mechanism: By actively segregating ions—keeping Sodium (Na⁺) outside and Potassium (K⁺) inside—the cell creates an electrical tension between two poles.
biological_function: Establishes the 'Resting Potential' required for a neuron to fire an Action Potential on command.

title: Depolarization
definition: A sudden change within a cell during which the cell undergoes a dramatic shift in electric charge distribution.
mechanism: Voltage-gated Sodium channels pop open, allowing a rush of positive Na⁺ ions into the negative interior, flipping the charge from -70mV toward +30mV.
biological_function: This is the actual 'firing' of the thought—the moment the electrical signal is committed to the journey.

title: Repolarization
definition: The stage of an action potential in which the cell's membrane potential is restored to its negative resting state.
mechanism: Sodium channels close and Potassium (K⁺) channels open, allowing positive ions to exit the cell, dropping the internal voltage back down to its negative baseline.
biological_function: Prevents the neuron from staying 'stuck' in a firing state, allowing the system to clear and prepare for the next focused intent.

title: Endoplasmic Reticulum (ER)
definition: A multifunctional organelle that serves as the primary storage site for intracellular Calcium.
mechanism: When triggered by ACh, the ER opens its gates, flooding the cell with Calcium to activate metabolic enzymes like eNOS.
biological_function: Acts as an amplifier, turning a small chemical signal into a massive metabolic shift.

title: Electrical Gradient
definition: The difference in electrical charge between the inside and the outside of a neuron's membrane.
mechanism: By keeping a high concentration of positive Sodium (Na+) outside and a negative charge inside (-70mV), the cell creates an 'electrical pull' that forces ions to rush in the moment a channel opens.
biological_function: Provides the stored potential energy necessary to trigger an instantaneous Action Potential (the spark) without needing to generate energy from scratch every time.

title: Neuron
definition: The specialized cell of the nervous system designed to transmit information.
mechanism: Uses a combination of internal electrical pulses and external chemical messengers to bridge long distances in the body.
biological_function: The primary building block of thought, sensation, and movement.

title: Dendrite
definition: The branch-like extensions of a neuron that receive incoming signals.
mechanism: Covered in receptors that catch neurotransmitters from neighboring cells, converting chemical keys into electrical nudges.
biological_function: The 'Input' stage where the neuron listens to the surrounding environment.

title: Soma (Cell Body)
definition: The central hub of the neuron containing the nucleus and organelles.
mechanism: Sums up all the incoming electrical 'whispers' (EPSPs) from the dendrites to see if they are strong enough to reach the threshold.
biological_function: Maintains the cell's life and acts as the gatekeeper for signal transmission.

title: Axon
definition: A long, slender projection that conducts electrical impulses away from the cell body.
mechanism: Propagates the action potential over long distances via a wave of opening and closing ion channels.
biological_function: The 'Output' cable that carries the command from the brain to the target tissue.

title: Synaptic Vesicle
definition: Small, membrane-bound bubbles located in the axon terminal.
mechanism: Store neurotransmitters and dock at the cell membrane to release their contents into the synapse when triggered by calcium.
biological_function: Protects reactive chemicals like Glutamate until the exact moment they are needed for signaling.

title: Threshold Potential
definition: The specific voltage level (-55mV) required to trigger an action potential.
mechanism: When the cell's internal charge rises to this level, it causes voltage-gated sodium channels to pop open simultaneously.
biological_function: Acts as a filter to prevent random 'noise' or weak thoughts from accidentally triggering a physical response.

title: Cholinergic System
definition: The network of neurons and pathways that primarily use Acetylcholine (ACh) to communicate.
mechanism: Modulates the 'leakiness' of neurons to sharpen signals and improve the signal-to-noise ratio.
biological_function: Essential for attention, learning, and directing focused intent to specific body parts.

title: AMPA Receptor
definition: A specific type of receptor on the dendrite that responds to Glutamate.
mechanism: When Glutamate binds, the receptor changes shape to let positive Sodium (Na+) rush into the cell.
biological_function: Responsible for the fast, initial 'spark' of a signal in the receiving neuron.

title: Glutamate
definition: The most abundant excitatory neurotransmitter in the vertebrate nervous system.
mechanism: Binds to receptors on the postsynaptic neuron to increase its internal positivity.
biological_function: The primary chemical used to relay 'active' information across the gap between neurons.

title: Excitatory Postsynaptic Potential (EPSP)
definition: A temporary increase in the neuron's internal voltage that brings it closer to the threshold.
mechanism: Created by the localized influx of positive ions through receptors like AMPA.
biological_function: A sub-threshold signal that must be combined with others to successfully 'fire' the neuron.

title: Action Potential
definition: A rapid, self-propagating electrical impulse that travels along the axon.
mechanism: A chain reaction of sodium rushing in and potassium rushing out, creating a spike of energy.
biological_function: The fundamental unit of communication that allows the brain to send 'real-time' commands to the hand.

title: Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)
definition: The network of nerves outside the brain and spinal cord.
mechanism: Relays sensory data up to the brain and motor commands down to the muscles and organs.
biological_function: Connects the 'Command Center' (Brain) to the 'Hardware' (Body).

title: Voltage-Gated Calcium Channels
definition: Protein tunnels in the cell membrane that open specifically in response to a change in voltage.
mechanism: When the action potential hits the terminal, these gates detect the positivity and open to let Calcium (Ca2+) flood in.
biological_function: Translates an electrical signal into a chemical action (the release of neurotransmitters).

title: Acetylcholine (ACh)
definition: A versatile neurotransmitter used in both the brain and the body's peripheral tissues.
mechanism: In the hand, it bridges the gap between the nerve terminal and the blood vessel or muscle tissue.
biological_function: Directly triggers the physical changes like vasodilation (blood flow) or muscle contraction.

title: Synaptotagmin
definition: A protein found on the surface of synaptic vesicles that acts as a calcium sensor.
mechanism: When calcium levels rise, it grabs the calcium ions and instantly signals the SNARE proteins to fuse the vesicle to the membrane.
biological_function: Ensures the chemical 'message' is released at the exact microsecond the electrical spark arrives.

title: Muscarinic Receptors
definition: A class of G-protein coupled receptors that respond to Acetylcholine.
mechanism: Once ACh binds, these receptors trigger internal signaling cascades (like releasing calcium from the ER).
biological_function: Regulates 'slow' biological responses like vessel relaxation and heart rate adjustment.

title: ATP (Adenosine Triphosphate)
definition: The primary energy currency of the cell.
mechanism: Stores energy in high-energy phosphate bonds; when a bond is broken, it releases the power needed for pumps and enzymes to work.
biological_function: Fueling the 'reset' of neurons and the physical work of vasodilation.

title: Calmodulin
definition: A calcium-binding messenger protein found in all eukaryotic cells.
mechanism: Once it binds to four Calcium ions, it changes its shape to 'hug' and activate other enzymes like eNOS.
biological_function: Translates the presence of Calcium into a signal for the cell to start producing gas.

title: eNOS (Endothelial Nitric Oxide Synthase)
definition: An enzyme located in the lining of blood vessels.
mechanism: Takes L-arginine and Oxygen and chemically converts them into Nitric Oxide gas when activated by Calmodulin.
biological_function: The key catalyst for increasing blood flow and creating the 'aura' effect.

title: Caveolin-1
definition: A scaffolding protein that keeps certain enzymes in an 'off' state.
mechanism: Physically binds to eNOS to prevent it from producing gas until the Calcium-Calmodulin complex pulls it away.
biological_function: Prevents the body from wasting energy or dilating blood vessels when no signal is present.

title: cGMP (Cyclic Guanosine Monophosphate)
definition: A 'second messenger' molecule that carries signals inside the smooth muscle cells.
mechanism: Produced when Nitric Oxide enters the muscle cell, it triggers the removal of calcium from the muscle fibers, causing them to relax.
biological_function: The final internal signal that forces a blood vessel to widen (dilate).

title: Nitric Oxide (NO)
definition: A short-lived, highly reactive gas molecule that acts as a signaling agent.
mechanism: Easily diffuses through cell membranes to tell nearby muscles to stop contracting.
biological_function: The direct cause of vasodilation and the increase in local metabolic activity.

title: Polarized
definition: The state of having two opposite charges separated by a barrier (the cell membrane).
mechanism: The neuron maintains a 'border' where the inside is electrically different from the outside, creating a state of readiness.
biological_function: Allows the neuron to act like a battery, storing potential energy that can be released instantly when a signal is needed.

title: The Negative-Positive Game
definition: The strategic balancing act of ions (Sodium and Potassium) to maintain a specific electrical charge.
mechanism: The cell uses energy to keep the inside at -70mV by pushing 3 Sodium ions out for every 2 Potassium ions it pulls in, creating an electrical 'pressure' across the membrane.
biological_function: Ensures the neuron is 'cocked' like a literal trigger, so the slightest focus or intent can set off a massive electrical chain reaction.
